title: Shift Handovers and Waste Tracking: The Gaps Between Shifts
subtitle: Most operational failures do not happen during shifts. They happen between them — in the gaps where information gets lost.

toc.label: Waste Tracking as a Daily Habit

metrics.label: target waste as percentage of revenue

sections.heading: Where Shifts Go Wrong
sections.body:
The most dangerous moment in a cafe's day is not the morning rush. It is the handover — the ten minutes when one shift leaves and another arrives. Information that lives in one person's head walks out the door, and the incoming team starts from zero.

Common handover failures:

**The fridge that failed.** The morning team noticed the walk-in fridge temperature was slightly high at 7am. They adjusted the thermostat and it came back down by 8am. They did not write it down. The afternoon team does not check. By 3pm, the fridge is at 9°C and the stock inside has been in the danger zone for hours. Nobody knows the morning team already spotted a problem.

**The angry customer.** A customer complained about a wrong order at 11am. The morning shift lead spoke to them and promised a free coffee on their next visit. The customer returns at 2pm. The afternoon team knows nothing about the promise. The customer feels lied to.

**The missing prep.** The morning team ran out of soup at noon and did not have time to make more. They planned to leave a note for the afternoon team. They forgot the note. The afternoon team discovers the gap when a customer orders soup at 1:30pm.

**The broken equipment.** The dishwasher started making a strange noise at 10am. The morning team worked around it. Nobody told anyone. The afternoon team runs it until it breaks completely at 4pm. Now there are no clean cups during evening service.

Every one of these failures has the same root cause: information was not transferred from one shift to another. The solution is not better people or better memory. It is a system that forces the handover to happen, with the same structure, every time.

Most cafes rely on verbal handovers: a quick chat between the outgoing and incoming shift leads. Verbal handovers miss things. They are incomplete, inconsistent, and unverifiable. You cannot prove what was said, and you cannot check what was forgotten.

sections.heading: The Structured Handover Protocol
sections.body:
A structured handover takes five minutes and uses a written template. The outgoing shift lead completes it before they leave. The incoming shift lead reads it, asks questions, and signs it. The completed form stays in the daily file.

Use T089-01 (Shift Handover Template):

**Section 1: Operations status**
- Equipment issues: any faults, workarounds, or repairs needed
- Stock issues: items 86'd, low stock warnings, deliveries expected
- Fridge and freezer temperatures: current readings and any anomalies
- Prep status: what was completed, what was not, what needs doing

**Section 2: Customer issues**
- Complaints received: what happened, what was promised, who the customer was
- Positive feedback: anything worth noting for team morale
- Special requests: pre-orders, reservations, catering pickups

**Section 3: Team issues**
- Staff performance: anyone struggling, anyone excelling
- Absences or late arrivals: impact on service and any cover arranged
- Morale: anything the incoming lead should be aware of

**Section 4: Numbers**
- Revenue so far today
- Transaction count
- Waste logged
- Any cash discrepancies

**Section 5: Priorities for next shift**
- The three most important things the incoming team needs to focus on

The handover conversation should follow the template, not replace it. The outgoing lead walks the incoming lead through the form, highlights the critical items, and answers questions. Then both sign it.

**Making it stick:**

The handover is not optional. It happens at the same time, in the same place, every shift change. The incoming lead does not start managing until they have received the handover. If the outgoing lead leaves without completing it, the manager follows up the same day.

Review handover forms weekly. Look for recurring issues — the same equipment problem noted three days in a row means it is not being fixed. The same stock outage every Tuesday means the ordering process is broken. The handover form is a diagnostic tool, not just a communication one.

sections.heading: Waste Tracking as a Daily Habit
sections.body:
Waste tracking is one of the highest-return habits a cafe can adopt. Every item you throw away was paid for. Knowing exactly what you waste, and why, gives you the information to stop it.

Use T092-01 (Daily Waste Tracker) throughout the day — not as an end-of-day memory exercise.

**The tracking discipline:**

Every time food or drink is discarded, it is logged immediately. Not at the end of the shift. Not from memory. At the moment it happens. Keep the waste tracker on a clipboard next to the bin. Make logging waste as automatic as logging a temperature check.

**What to log:**

- Item name
- Quantity
- Time
- Reason (expired, over-prepped, customer return, dropped, poor quality)
- Estimated cost
- Who logged it

**Analysing the data:**

Review the waste log weekly. You are looking for patterns, not individual items.

- **Consistent over-prep:** If you throw away three chicken sandwiches every day, your par level is three too high. Reduce it.
- **Time-of-day patterns:** If most waste happens at close, your afternoon prep quantities are too high. Reduce the afternoon batch.
- **Day-of-week patterns:** If Mondays consistently produce more waste than Fridays, your Monday ordering or prep is not adjusted for lower Monday demand.
- **Category concentration:** If most waste is in one category (cakes, salads, batch brew), focus your reduction efforts there.

**The waste target:**

Total waste should be below three percent of revenue. Between three and five percent indicates room for improvement. Above five percent is a significant margin leak that demands immediate attention.

To put this in context: a cafe turning over five thousand pounds per week with a five percent waste rate is throwing away two hundred and fifty pounds every week — thirteen thousand pounds per year. Reducing that to three percent saves five thousand two hundred pounds annually. That is a meaningful sum, achieved not by working harder but by tracking and adjusting.

**Team engagement:**

Share waste data with the team. Post the weekly waste total in the staff area. When waste drops, acknowledge it. When it rises, investigate together. Waste reduction is a team effort — the person prepping, the person ordering, and the person deciding what to sell all play a role.

sections.heading: Seating Turnover Management
sections.body:
Your revenue is limited by three things: the number of customers, the average spend per customer, and the number of times each seat turns over. Most cafe owners focus on the first two and ignore the third.

Seating turnover — the number of times each seat is occupied and vacated during a trading day — determines your capacity utilisation. A cafe with thirty seats and an average dwell time of ninety minutes during peak has a maximum of two turns per peak period. If dwell time drops to forty-five minutes, capacity doubles.

**Measuring turnover:**

Use T096-01 (Seating Turnover Monitor) to count covers per seat during peak periods. You do not need technology for this — a simple tally during the lunch rush gives you the data.

**Factors that affect turnover:**

- **Seating type.** Comfortable sofas encourage long stays. High stools and bench seating encourage shorter visits. Your seating mix should reflect your business model. A cafe that depends on high turnover during lunch should not fill the space with deep armchairs.
- **Wi-Fi.** Free Wi-Fi attracts laptop workers who stay for hours, occupying tables and buying one coffee. If this is not your target customer, consider time-limiting Wi-Fi (ninety minutes) or restricting it during peak hours.
- **Power sockets.** Accessible power sockets signal "stay as long as you like." If you want higher turnover during peak, limit socket access to off-peak hours or specific zones.
- **Table clearing.** A table with dirty cups signals "occupied" even when the customer has left. Clear tables within two minutes of a customer leaving. This sounds obvious but is regularly neglected during busy periods.

**Balancing turnover and experience:**

There is a tension between maximising turnover and providing a relaxed experience. Rushing customers out damages loyalty. The solution is not to rush anyone — it is to design the environment so that natural dwell times align with your revenue needs.

During off-peak periods, long stays are valuable — a customer spending three hours nursing a coffee and a cake is better than an empty table. During peak, the same behaviour blocks revenue. Design your space and policies to flex: cosy corners for lingerers, quick-service zones for grab-and-go, and clear signage that manages expectations during busy periods.

sections.heading: Delivery Integration
sections.body:
If you use third-party delivery platforms, integrating them into your operations is essential. A delivery order that arrives on a separate tablet, is manually entered into the POS, and prepared as an interruption to in-house orders creates chaos during busy periods.

**The integration challenge:**

Delivery platforms (Deliveroo, Uber Eats, Just Eat) each provide their own tablet, their own notification sound, and their own order format. If you are on all three platforms plus handling in-house orders, your kitchen is managing four separate order streams. This is a recipe for errors, delays, and frustration.

**Solutions:**

- **POS integration.** The best solution is to integrate delivery platforms directly with your POS. Orders from Deliveroo, Uber Eats, and Just Eat appear on the same screen and in the same ticket queue as in-house orders. Platforms like Deliverect, Otter, or your POS provider's own integration handle this. The cost is typically a monthly fee per platform, but the operational saving is substantial.
- **Aggregator tablets.** If full POS integration is not available, use an aggregator that consolidates all delivery platforms onto a single tablet. This reduces the number of screens to monitor from three to one.
- **Dedicated station.** If integration is not possible, designate one team member during peak hours to manage delivery tablets. They receive orders, call them to the kitchen, and manage rider handoffs. This is labour-intensive but prevents delivery orders from disrupting in-house service.

**Rider management:**

Designate a collection point for delivery riders away from the customer counter. Riders arriving at the main till create congestion and confuse in-house customers. A shelf or section of the pass marked "Delivery collection" keeps the flow separate.

Label delivery orders clearly with the platform name and order number. Riders need to find the right order quickly — misidentified orders create delays, complaints, and potential food safety issues (wrong items delivered to wrong customers).

**Menu management:**

Keep your delivery menu tightly curated. Not everything on your in-house menu works for delivery. Remove items that do not travel well, items with complex preparation that slows the kitchen, and items with low margins that are further compressed by platform commission. Review your delivery menu monthly based on sales data and customer feedback.

sections.heading: Deep Clean and Maintenance Scheduling
sections.body:
Maintenance that is not scheduled does not happen. And maintenance that does not happen becomes a repair — which costs more, disrupts service, and always happens at the worst possible moment.

**The maintenance schedule:**

Use T101-01 (Maintenance Schedule Template) to create a rolling maintenance calendar that covers daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annual tasks.

**Daily maintenance (built into shift routines):**
- Backflush espresso machine
- Clean grinder exterior and dosing area
- Wipe down all surfaces and equipment
- Check fridge and freezer temperatures
- Empty grease traps if applicable
- Check handwash stations are stocked

**Weekly maintenance (assigned to a specific day):**
- Deep clean grinder (disassemble, brush, reassemble)
- Descale kettles and batch brewers
- Clean fridge interiors
- Check water filter indicators
- Clean extractor fan filters
- Test dishwasher wash and rinse temperatures

**Monthly maintenance:**
- Replace water filters (or per manufacturer schedule)
- Inspect espresso machine group head gaskets
- Service dishwasher (spray arms, filters, drain)
- Check all plumbing for leaks
- Test emergency lighting and fire alarm
- PAT test any new electrical equipment

**Quarterly:**
- Professional espresso machine service
- HVAC filter cleaning or replacement
- Deep clean of extraction and ventilation systems
- Full pest inspection
- Review and update all cleaning chemical stocks

**Annual:**
- Gas safety certificate
- EICR (Electrical Installation Condition Report)
- Fire risk assessment review
- Insurance review
- Full equipment audit — assess condition and replacement timeline

**Tracking and accountability:**

Print the maintenance schedule and post it in the back of house. Each task has a responsible person and a sign-off space. The manager reviews completion weekly. Incomplete maintenance is investigated — not to blame, but to understand why it was missed and how to prevent recurrence.

The cost of preventive maintenance is always less than the cost of reactive repair. A professional espresso machine service costs a few hundred pounds per year. An emergency callout because the machine failed during Saturday morning service costs double — plus the lost revenue from the hours it was down.
